ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (hot pot food product) is the closest parent class available. No specific Tom Yum steamboat set entry exists in FoodOn; closest_parent match used and documented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Tom Yum steamboat set code. A0CKW (mixed composite dish) is the closest defensible parent anchor. Documented as closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for steamboat or hot pot set dishes as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One individual steamboat set for 1 person typically comprises approximately 600g of combined broth, protein ingredients (seafood, tofu), vegetables, and noodles as served in Singaporean hawker centres and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Steamboat (Tom Yum) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Steamboat (Tom Yum, 1 pax), cr_id F08010059. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; glass noodles / mixed hot pot analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum steamboat set. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli, GI ~26–39 per University of Sydney GI Database) combined with mixed vegetables and protein ingredients that moderate glycaemic response. The composite dish GI is estimated conservatively at 45 to account for variability in noodle type and broth carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 37.8g carbohydrate in 600g serving) / 100 ≈ 17.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 616mg per 100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g high-sodium threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid drinking the tom yum broth, request reduced fish sauce and no added salt, and choose plain ingredients such as vegetables, tofu, and lean fish while avoiding processed fishballs or luncheon meat to limit sodium intake to a safer level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (6.3g/100g) with low sugar (1.48g/100g); glass noodles provide a lower GI carbohydrate base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in moderate portions, favouring glass noodles over white rice or instant noodles, limiting dipping sauces with added sugar, and pairing with lean protein and vegetables to moderate the overall glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (616mg/100g) poses a cardiovascular risk; fat content is moderate at 4.67g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~1.2g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, choose lean seafood and tofu over processed meats, and request reduced fish sauce to lower the overall cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (lemongrass, galangal, chili) are beneficial, but high sodium and nightshade ingredients may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in tom yum spices but should monitor personal tolerance to chili and other nightshades, request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling, and avoid processed add-ins high in sodium and saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamboat Set (Tom Yum, 1 Pax) is an individual hot pot meal served in Singaporean hawker centres and restaurants, featuring a spicy-sour Thai-style tom yum broth as the cooking medium. The broth is prepared with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chili, fish sauce, and lime juice, delivering a characteristic bold, tangy, and aromatic flavour profile. The set typically includes assorted proteins such as prawns, fish slices, and tofu, alongside mushrooms, leafy vegetables, corn, and glass noodles or rice vermicelli that are cooked tableside in the simmering broth. Per 100g, the dish provides 82 kcal, 3.99g protein, 4.67g fat, 6.3g carbohydrate, 1.48g sugar, 0.61g dietary fibre, and 616mg sodium. The high sodium content (616mg/100g) is a primary clinical concern, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension unless broth consumption is minimised and fish sauce is reduced. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is moderately suitable due to its low sugar content and the lower glycaemic index of glass noodles compared to white rice noodles, though portion control and avoidance of high-sugar dipping sauces are advised. The tom yum spices contain polyphenol-rich anti-inflammatory compounds beneficial for general health and potentially for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, though nightshade sensitivity should be monitored. This dish is a popular communal and individual dining option in Singapore, reflecting the strong Thai culinary influence on the local food culture.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010059) with a confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on glass noodles GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, documented as such. Saturated fat is estimated from typical tom yum steamboat composition as the HPB entry does not report this sub-fraction; this is the only estimated value.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is estimated from typical tom yum steamboat composition based on HPB comparable mixed dish entries; the HPB F08010059 record does not report saturated fat as a separate field. This should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite tom yum steamboat set are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli) as the dominant carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 4. The dish is classified as Thai-influenced Singaporean cuisine; Thai-script local name is included given the Thai origin of the tom yum broth. 5. Halal status is conditionally true assuming seafood and tofu ingredients are sourced from halal-certified suppliers; fish sauce brand may affect halal certification — consumers should verify. 6. Ontology match types set to closest_parent for FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact codes exist for this specific composite dish.
